turns.text: Hello Dahlia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. It’s funny, sitting here in the waiting room at the Southbridge clinic, I feel like a completely different person than I was with my first pregnancy. Back then, everything was just… magical. I didn't worry about anything. Now? I’m twenty-eight, juggling a toddler at home, and I feel this constant, heavy responsibility.

I think my background makes it hard to just "be." I’m constantly researching things—like, for months, I was spiraling about MMR immunity. I knew the data, I knew I was protected, but I couldn't stop checking the grocery store aisles for anyone who looked even slightly sick. It’s exhausting, honestly. My midwife, Sarah, she’s so patient with me, even when I show up with a list of questions about screening protocols that I’ve probably asked twice already. I know she sees me as "low risk," and I try to tell myself that, but the fear of something going wrong with this baby... it’s always just sitting there, right behind my ribs. 

I just want to reach that finish line, you know? I want to stop being a researcher of my own symptoms and a nervous tracker of fetal movement and just be a mom to two healthy kids. It’s a lot to hold onto all at once.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

Honestly, I spent way too much time on my birth plan. I had this laminated folder, color-coded and everything. I wanted it to be this roadmap for my care—I researched all the evidence-based practices for labor, interventions, and immediate postpartum care because I wanted to feel like I had some semblance of control in a situation that is inherently unpredictable. 

I really thought the staff would view it as a collaborative tool, but the reality was... different. When I handed it over, I felt like they were just being polite, kind of nodding along while checking off their own internal boxes. It wasn't that they were rude—they were professional—but it felt like they were looking at a document I’d spent weeks perfecting as just another piece of paperwork to get through.

And once labor actually hit? All that focus on the details just evaporated. It’s hard to advocate for the specific cadence of delayed cord clamping or neonatal procedures when you’re in the middle of a contraction. I found myself in this weird tug-of-war, trying to stay present for the baby while simultaneously trying to remember if I’d documented my preferences clearly enough for the nurses to actually follow them. It felt less like a partnership and more like I was fighting to keep my voice heard above the hum of the monitors.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I had this idea that my midwife and I would have these calm, rational conversations about pain management—a "menu" of sorts, where we’d weigh the pros and cons of nitrous oxide versus an epidural well before the intensity set in. I thought the support would be centered on my autonomy; that no matter what I chose, I’d be encouraged and informed without any pressure.

The reality, though, was way less clinical and way more primal. When the contractions really ramped up, that "menu" just didn't exist in my head anymore. I felt like I was in a tunnel. I’d researched the efficacy of different interventions until I was blue in the face, but when you're physically shaking and the pain is all-consuming, you stop being a researcher. You just need someone to tell you it’s going to be okay.

The staff kept asking, "What do you want to do?" and honestly, it felt like an interrogation instead of support. It was overwhelming. I felt a weird mix of guilt—like if I asked for an epidural, I was "failing" my plan, even though I knew logically that wasn't true. I wanted them to guide me, to read my cues, but it felt like they were just waiting for me to give them an order. It was isolating.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

It wasn’t so much that options were locked away from me, but more that the environment itself felt like a barrier. I really wanted to labor in the water—I had read so many studies about the benefits of hydrotherapy for relaxation and pain reduction. I kept asking about the tub, but there was always a reason it wasn't quite "ready" or the monitoring requirements made it complicated. It felt like every time I tried to lean into a more natural, low-intervention comfort measure, there was a bureaucratic hurdle or a "protocol" that pushed me back toward the bed and the monitors.

The monitors were the biggest culprit, really. Because I was being tracked so closely, the thought of being untethered—moving freely, getting in the water, changing positions—felt like I was fighting against the hospital’s way of doing things. I wanted to be mobile, but the technology kept me anchored. It made me feel like my body was a piece of equipment that needed to be plugged in rather than a woman in labor. It’s frustrating because I came in wanting to trust my instincts, but the systems in place seemed designed to favor the ease of the medical staff over the needs of my body. It made me feel like I had to apologize for wanting a different kind of experience.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I think I expected the hospital to feel like a sanctuary—or at least a place where people were genuinely happy for me. Since I’m a "low-risk" pregnancy, I imagined I’d be welcomed with warmth, like they were excited to help me bring my second child into the world. I pictured the room being quiet, maybe some dim lighting, and a staff that felt more like a team rooting for my success.

When I actually walked through those sliding doors, though, it felt more like checking into a high-security airport. There’s that sterile, clinical hum that hits you the second you step inside—that smell of industrial disinfectant that triggers this immediate, visceral anxiety. Everything felt incredibly transactional. Nobody looked at me as a person bringing life into the world; I was just a chart number, a bed assignment, another patient to be processed.

The room itself was cold, and honestly, quite bright. It didn't feel like a space meant for comfort or the vulnerability of labor. Every time I looked at the equipment—the monitors, the plastic IV setups, the stark white walls—I felt less like a mother and more like a subject to be managed. It wasn't hostile, but it was profoundly impersonal. I wanted to feel cared for, but instead, I just felt monitored.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I had this romanticized notion that if things veered off course, we’d pause. I pictured my midwife leaning in, making eye contact, and explaining the risks, the benefits, and the alternatives in a way that respected my intelligence. I expected a collaborative, human conversation where my "why" was just as important as the clinical "what."

In the heat of it, though, that shared decision-making just vanished. When the baby's heart rate dipped and they suggested pitocin to speed things up, it didn’t feel like a discussion—it felt like a directive. The nurse brought it up almost as a foregone conclusion, delivered with a sense of urgency that didn’t leave room for me to process the trade-offs. It felt like I was being told, not asked.

It’s hard to advocate for yourself when you’re terrified and the clock feels like it’s ticking louder than your own heartbeat. I felt like a passenger in my own birth. They were doing things *to* me, not *with* me. Even though I knew they were trying to keep us safe, the lack of agency was jarring. I felt stripped of the one thing I wanted most: the ability to understand why we were pivoting and to truly consent, rather than just complying because I felt I had no other choice. It left me feeling really small in a moment where I needed to feel strong.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I expected my midwife to be my anchor. Because we’d spent months talking through my anxieties and my research, I really believed she would be the one to bridge that gap between the clinical "stuff" and the emotional reality of what I was going through. I wanted someone to just acknowledge the fear—to look at me, not the monitor, and say, "I know you're doing hard work, and you're safe."

But the reality was that she was pulled in so many directions. Even when she was in the room, her attention was constantly fractured. She’d be trying to offer a kind word while simultaneously adjusting a drip or checking a printout from the monitor. It felt like her clinical role was constantly competing with her ability to just *be* with me. 

There was this one nurse who was lovely, but she kept reminding me to "breathe through the peak," which I knew how to do, but it felt so mechanical. I didn't need a coach; I needed a witness. I wanted to feel like my experience mattered to them as a human life, not just as a successful delivery outcome. By the end, I felt like I was just another patient they were trying to move through a shift. I left feeling like I’d done the work alone, even though the room was full of people.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

There was one moment that really sticks with me, even months later. I was in the middle of transition—that point where you think you literally cannot do another second of it. My husband was doing his best, but he looked just as exhausted and terrified as I felt. I remember looking up at the nurse who had been monitoring me for hours; she was busy adjusting the settings on the fetal heart monitor, her eyes glued to the screen, completely focused on the machine’s readout. 

I reached out, wanting to just grab someone’s hand—not for medical guidance, but just to tether myself to the earth. She didn't notice. She just kept tapping the screen, murmuring something about a "deceleration." In that second, the silence in the room felt deafening. I wasn't Dahlia anymore; I was a data point, a set of vitals, a potential complication. I felt so profoundly alone, even with three people in the room. 

I realized then that all my preparation, my research, and my "birth plan" couldn't protect me from the loneliness of being a patient in a system that prioritizes numbers over people. I remember squeezing my eyes shut and just sobbing, not because of the pain, but because I felt like I was disappearing. I needed a person, and I was given a technician.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had this vision of a "golden hour"—that serene, quiet space where the world stops and it’s just the three of us. I expected the baby to be placed directly on my chest, skin-to-skin immediately, while the medical staff faded into the background to let us bond. I imagined my husband being the one to announce her birth, and that any routine checks would wait at least an hour. I wanted it to feel like an intimate transition into motherhood.

The reality was much more fragmented. As soon as she was out, there was this flurry of activity that felt anything but peaceful. They took her briefly to the warmer—they said it was for routine assessment because of the heart rate dip earlier, but it felt like they were just following a checklist. I stayed on the bed, feeling exposed and confused, while a few different people moved around the room checking vitals.

I didn't get that immediate, uninterrupted contact. It was more like, "Here, you can hold her for a second," but then there was a constant interruption for measurements or injections. I felt like I was watching my own birth from a distance. I wanted to be the first one to truly *welcome* her, but the medical machinery took priority. It wasn’t the calm, sacred start I’d spent so long visualizing; it was a process, and I was just the final station.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

It’s the silence—or the lack of it—that haunts me. I keep thinking about how, after all those months of tracking her heart rate on monitors and obsessing over my own immunity, the very first thing I heard wasn't her cry, but the clinical, rhythmic "beep-beep-beep" of the equipment they had her connected to across the room.

I remember lying there, waiting for that weight of her on my chest, and instead, I heard a nurse say something about "getting her stable." That phrase just sits in my gut. I wasn't allowed to be the one to provide that stability; the machines and the protocols were deemed more capable than I was. 

I still have this recurring image: I’m reaching out, and my hands are just empty, hovering over the bed while they’re busy with their swabs and their tape measures. I know, logically, that they were just doing their jobs and that she was healthy, but I feel like I lost that first, vital connection—that moment where she was supposed to recognize me as her mother, not just another patient in the maternity wing. I wasn't a mother holding her baby; I was a recipient of a medical service. I just wanted to be a mom, and I feel like I missed the first chapter of being her mom because I was sidelined by the very system meant to keep us safe.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I thought I’d be prepared. I’d read all the blogs and the clinical summaries about the healing process, so I expected the basics—the fatigue, the recovery from the physical trauma, the hormonal shifts. I think I subconsciously assumed that because I was “low risk” and had survived the birth, the recovery would just be a standard, linear healing trajectory. I figured I’d be tired, sure, but I expected that by the time I was home, I’d be easing back into my role as a mom to two.

The reality was so much heavier. It wasn't just the physical healing; it was the lingering, raw feeling of having my boundaries pushed during the delivery. Every time I looked at my body, I didn’t just see a woman recovering from childbirth—I saw the sites of where I felt overruled. 

I’m exhausted in a way that sleep doesn't fix. The physical aches are one thing, but there’s this persistent, background hum of frustration that I’m still processing. It’s hard to "rest" when your mind keeps replaying the moments where you felt small. I’m doing the sitz baths, I’m taking the vitamins, I’m doing the "right" things, but no one gives you a protocol for how to heal from feeling like a spectator in your own life. My body is mending, but my confidence in the medical system—and, strangely, in my own intuition—is taking a lot longer to bounce back. It’s a slow, quiet, and honestly lonely kind of work.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I expected breastfeeding to be... well, natural. That’s what every pamphlet in the office emphasized. I’d read all the literature on latching, the importance of colostrum, and the "biological imperative." I assumed it would be an intuitive extension of our bond, another way for me to reclaim that closeness I felt I’d lost during those first chaotic hours in the hospital. I thought if I just followed the "best practices," it would be seamless.

The reality was a frantic, stressful performance. In those first weeks, it felt less like feeding my daughter and more like a high-stakes exam I was failing. My supply was slow to come in, and instead of calm support, I was bombarded with varying advice from nurses and consultants who all seemed to have different, contradictory "protocols." One said this, the other said that—it was like being back at the hospital, being told what to do without being listened to.

I was physically drained from recovery, and the constant, clicking noise of the pump became the soundtrack of my misery. I’d sit there at 3 AM, sobbing, feeling like I was once again fighting a system that prioritized output and efficiency over my mental health. I’m doing it, but it doesn't feel "magical." It feels like another chore on a checklist, a lingering reminder that my body is still being treated like a utility to be managed.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

Yes, absolutely. The support I was offered was incredibly task-oriented. I had lactation consultants and nurses coming at me with scales and charts, focusing entirely on the "math"—How many ounces? How many minutes? Is the weight gain on track? They were treating my baby’s intake like a data set to be optimized. 

What I actually needed was someone to acknowledge how much of a psychological toll this was taking on me. I didn't need another lecture on latching techniques—I had already memorized those from the books. I needed someone to sit with me and say, "I see that you're exhausted, and it’s okay if this feels hard." I needed someone to address the *human* side of the struggle, to validate that my anxiety wasn't just a clinical problem to be solved, but a feeling that mattered.

It felt like they were trying to fix a machine instead of mothering a mother. Every time they pulled out that scale, I felt like I was being graded. It wasn't support; it was surveillance. It made me feel like if I couldn't "perform" at breastfeeding, I was failing as a mom. I just wanted someone to hold space for the confusion and the overwhelming pressure I was feeling, but instead, they just kept giving me more homework.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I expected the "postpartum glow"—or at least a baseline of contentment. I thought, naively, that once the "threat" of the pregnancy was over—once the baby was safely in my arms—the anxiety would evaporate. I told myself that the obsession with MMR vaccines and fetal movement counts was just a pregnancy symptom, something that would resolve the moment I was holding a healthy baby.

The reality? The anxiety just shifted shape. It didn't go away; it morphed into a terrifying, constant vigilance. I’m not just the "researcher of my own symptoms" anymore; I’m the researcher of every tiny cough, every shift in breathing, every waking moment of my daughter’s life. I find myself staring at her while she sleeps, checking for movement, terrified that if I look away, something will go wrong. 

It feels like my nervous system is still stuck in that hospital room, waiting for the next intervention or the next complication. I feel a weird, heavy detachment, like I’m observing my life from a few feet away. I love her so much it hurts, but that love is wrapped in this paralyzing fear that I’m not doing it right, or that I’m missing something critical. I’m supposed to be "a mom again," but I feel like I'm still trying to survive the birth, over and over in my head.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

Oh, absolutely. There is this invisible—but very rigid—script you’re expected to follow once you’re home with a healthy baby. You’re supposed to be in this state of radiant, exhausted bliss. People tell you, “You’re so lucky,” or “At least she’s healthy,” and they’re right, she *is* healthy, but that makes me feel even more guilty for feeling so… hollow.

If I try to voice that I’m struggling with the lingering trauma of how the birth went, I get these well-meaning but dismissive responses: “The important thing is she’s here,” or “Just focus on the now.” It feels like I’m being told my feelings are inconvenient or invalid because the "clinical outcome" was successful. 

Even from myself, there’s this constant pressure to “get over it.” I catch myself thinking, *Dahlia, you’re a grown woman, you’re an educated person, why can’t you just process this?* I feel like I’m not allowed to be sad or angry because I didn’t have a "traumatic birth" in the way people usually define it—I didn’t have an emergency C-section or a NICU stay. But I feel violated by the experience nonetheless, and having no space to put that grief makes it grow. I’m expected to perform this seamless transition into a mother of two, and any sign that I’m struggling feels like I’m breaking the social contract of motherhood.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I think I expected that becoming a mother for the second time would just be an expansion of who I already was. I pictured myself seamlessly integrating a second child into my identity—a "mother of two"—without losing the professional, capable version of myself that I’d worked so hard to build. I thought I would be the same person, just... more.

The reality has been more like a fragmentation. I feel like my identity has been hollowed out by the sheer volume of "mom" tasks—tracking, feeding, worrying—that I don't know where the professional, analytical me ends and the anxious, overwhelmed mother begins. When I was pregnant, I was a researcher of my own health; now, I’m a researcher of my children's health. I feel like I’ve lost the ability to be a person who exists outside of the safety and surveillance of my family. 

I’m struggling to find the "me" that isn't tethered to a monitor or a feeding schedule. I’m a mom, yes, but I feel like I’m a mom who’s been hollowed out by the system I had to navigate to get here. I’m trying to rebuild, but it’s hard when your days are defined by other people’s needs and the lingering fear that if you drop the ball, everything you’ve protected will shatter. I just want to feel like a whole person again.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

The part that’s been the hardest to hold onto is my sense of agency as an intellectual, capable woman. Before I had kids, I navigated the world by researching, analyzing, and then acting on that data. I felt confident that if I gathered enough information, I could handle whatever was thrown at me.

But the hospital experience fundamentally broke that trust. When I realized that my research, my "data," and my questions were treated as mere inconveniences or secondary to hospital protocol, it felt like the professional part of me was being erased. Now, when I’m at home, I find myself second-guessing every instinct I have because I’m so conditioned to wait for a "professional" or an "expert" to tell me if what I’m doing is correct. 

Socially, too, it’s hard. I don't feel like I can just hang out with friends anymore because I’m constantly scanning the room for germs or worried about the baby’s schedule. I used to be the person who brought ideas to a conversation, but now, the only thing I have the bandwidth to talk about is how much she ate or why she’s crying. It’s like motherhood has become a filter that everything else in my life has to pass through, and I’m losing the parts of me that aren’t just "mothering." I miss who I was before I became a patient.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected those check-ups to be a time for us to debrief—a safe, quiet space where I could process what happened in the delivery room, how my body felt, and how I was coping mentally. I thought the midwife would sit down and ask, "How are you *really* doing?" and we’d look at the whole picture: the physical healing, the breastfeeding struggles, and that pervasive, gnawing anxiety that I couldn't shake. I wanted to feel validated.

In practice? The appointments were five-minute, blink-and-you-missed-it, purely clinical checklists. They checked my blood pressure, asked if I was bleeding normally, maybe did a quick glance at the incision, and then turned to the baby. The entire focus was on my biological output and the baby's growth. There was no space for the "me" inside this shell. When I tried to bring up the fact that I wasn't sleeping because I was constantly obsessing over the baby's breathing, the response was, "That’s just new mom anxiety, it’ll pass."

It felt like they were clearing me for duty, not checking on my well-being. I left every single appointment feeling smaller and more isolated than when I walked in. It wasn't follow-up care; it was a compliance check. They fixed the physical, but the person who went through the trauma was completely ignored.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

Yes, there were. I remember mentioning, quite explicitly at my six-week check, that I was experiencing these intrusive, terrifying thoughts about the baby—not about *hurting* her, but about the world being fundamentally unsafe. I told my midwife that I couldn't stop replaying the moments in the hospital where I felt things were going wrong, and that I was struggling to trust my own instincts because the staff had made me feel like an amateur.

I wanted to talk about it. I wanted to see if there was some support for this specific kind of vigilance, because it felt pathological. But she just did that polite, tight-lipped smile and said, "That’s so normal for a new mom, especially one with your background. You're just very detail-oriented." 

That felt like such a brush-off. It minimized everything I was feeling into a personality trait rather than a mental health struggle. It wasn't "being detail-oriented"—it was a trauma response to being stripped of my agency in that delivery room. When they didn't dig deeper, it signaled to me that my mental health wasn't a priority, that as long as I was "functioning" and the baby was healthy, my suffering didn't "count." I left that office feeling like my inner life was invisible. It just reinforced that I was on my own.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I expected returning to work to be a return to "myself." I thought it would be the place where I could reclaim my brain, my professional confidence, and that part of me that isn't just a caregiver. I figured it would be an adjustment, sure, but I looked forward to the intellectual stimulation—the chance to be valued for my expertise rather than just my ability to follow medical protocols.

The reality was a jarring collision of worlds. I’m physically present at my desk, but mentally, I’m still a thousand miles away, scanning for signs of trouble at home. My brain feels like it’s been stretched too thin. I find myself constantly checking my phone, checking the daycare updates, and feeling this sharp, irrational guilt that I’m not there to monitor every single detail of her day. 

It’s exhausting to perform this "professional" role when I feel like I’m still internally vibrating with the trauma of the birth and the constant, high-stakes pressure of motherhood. I feel like a ghost in the office—people see me, but they don't see the woman who’s barely holding it together under the surface. I wanted work to be my anchor, but instead, it’s just one more place where I have to perform, and I’m already so, so tired of performing.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

Everything felt harder. It wasn't just the logistical nightmare of pumping at work—trying to find a private, quiet space that didn't feel like a medical utility closet—but the mental exhaustion of it all. I’d be in the middle of a project, and the physical need to pump would hit me, not just as a biological necessity but as a jarring reminder of my separation from her. It’s like the pump became this constant, humming symbol of my guilt.

Then there’s the childcare transition. Leaving her with someone else, even someone wonderful, felt like a betrayal of my own hyper-vigilance. I had researched every daycare protocol, every staff-to-child ratio, but the reality was that I was still terrified that I was handing over the responsibility of her safety to someone who didn't *know* her like I did.

My own health took a backseat, as always. I’m physically recovering, but the lack of sleep and the constant, background hum of anxiety about "did I do this right, is she okay, did I miss a sign" makes it hard to even focus on my work. I felt like I was failing at both—being a mother and being a professional—because I couldn't fully commit to either. It’s like I’m constantly juggling, and I’m just waiting for the moment when everything I’ve been trying to hold onto finally slips.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I expected us to be a seamless unit—a team. We’d discussed the division of labor so many times before the birth. I pictured us both leaning into the chaos together, him knowing exactly when to step in so I could breathe, and us finding these quiet, intentional moments to reconnect after the baby was down. I imagined a partnership built on shared observation and equal concern.

The reality has been much more… fractured. We both feel like we’re drowning, but in different ways. He’s trying to be supportive, but he didn’t experience the physical trauma or the feeling of being sidelined by the medical team. When I try to explain *why* I’m so anxious, or why the hospital experience still feels so raw, he tries to "fix" it by telling me that we’re safe now and that the baby is fine. It’s well-intentioned, but it feels like he’s invalidating the depth of what I’m feeling.

It’s created this distance between us. I feel like I’m carrying this invisible weight of hyper-vigilance alone, and he’s struggling to keep up with the daily demands of a toddler and an infant. We aren't really connecting; we’re just managing logistics. I miss the intimacy of us, but right now, there isn't room for "us"—there’s only room for the baby and the never-ending checklist of what needs to be done. We’re passing ships in the night, both exhausted, both trying so hard, but somehow missing each other entirely.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise was realizing how much of our intimacy was actually built on the assumption that we were on the same page—that we shared the same fears and the same view of the world. After the birth, I realized we were living in two different realities. 

He honestly believes that because the baby is healthy and at home, the "threat" is gone. He sees our life as a success story. I, on the other hand, am still living in that hospital room, physically and emotionally. I’m waiting for the next shoe to drop, and when he tells me to "just relax" or "stop worrying," it doesn't feel like support. It feels like he’s dismissing the very real, visceral trauma I carried home with me.

It surprised me how lonely that makes you feel—being with the person you love most in the world, yet feeling like you’re experiencing motherhood in total isolation. I thought we would be a team, but instead, I feel like I’m constantly trying to bring him into my internal world, and he’s constantly trying to pull me out of it into his, and neither of us is succeeding. We aren't fighting, exactly, but we aren't really *seeing* each other anymore. We’re just two people trying to survive the same day.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected maternity care to be a partnership. I genuinely believed that if I came to the table prepared—with my health background, my research, and my advocacy—the medical system would meet me in the middle. I expected them to value my perspective as the primary observer of my own body and my baby’s well-being. I thought the goal was to support me through a transition that was both physical and profoundly human, providing a safety net that allowed me to feel empowered.

The gap, looking back, is that I was given medical services, not human care. I received clinical excellence in terms of safety—my baby is healthy, and for that, I am grateful every single day—but I was completely stripped of my agency. They monitored my vitals, but they ignored my spirit. 

The biggest, most painful gap was the total lack of "witnessing." I didn't need them to agree with me on every point; I needed them to see the woman behind the chart. I needed them to understand that when I asked questions, it wasn't because I didn't trust their expertise, but because I was trying to tether myself to reality during a terrifying, vulnerable time. Instead, I was treated like a container to be managed. That cold, transactional approach left me feeling like I had to fight my own birth team to exist, and that is a wound that hasn't finished healing. I am a mother, but I feel like I'm still trying to reclaim the person I was before I walked through those hospital doors.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

If I could change one thing, it would be to fundamentally shift the system’s primary objective from "successful biological outcome" to "holistic well-being." Right now, the system is designed like a manufacturing line: you come in, you are processed, and the goal is just to get the product—the healthy baby—out the other side with minimal clinical error. 

I want a system that builds in *time* for connection. I want a model where the midwife or the doctor isn't just checking boxes on a tablet, but is actively tasked with being a witness to the mother's experience. This means dismantling the protocols that force us to be "plugged in" to monitors the second we walk through the doors. It means creating an environment where moving, breathing, and existing as a human being takes precedence over the ease of the medical staff. 

I’d love to see a system where the "birth plan" isn't a nuisance that they have to work around, but a foundational document for how that specific woman wants to be supported—not just physically, but emotionally. We need to stop treating maternity care as a series of interventions and start treating it as a sacred transition. I want a system that trusts a mother’s voice, acknowledges her research, and recognizes that her mental and emotional safety is just as critical as her physical safety.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think what I keep coming back to, and what I hope people like you really understand, is that "healthy baby, healthy mom" is not a complete definition of success. The system acts like that’s the finish line, and once you cross it, anything you’re feeling—the trauma, the loss of agency, the feeling of being discarded—is just collateral damage. But it’s not damage. It’s part of the birth. 

When I look at my daughter, I am overwhelmed with love, but there’s this shadow over that joy because I wasn't allowed to be the lead character in her arrival. I feel like I have to carry the weight of that experience in silence because if I speak up, I’m told I’m ungrateful. 

I’d just want people to know that when a mother asks a question for the third time, or when she seems "too anxious," she’s not being difficult. She’s trying to stay in her own body. She’s trying to stay connected to herself while the system tries to pull her out of it. If we could just shift the culture to be a little bit more curious about the woman in the bed—to ask "how are you holding this?" instead of just checking the vitals—I think fewer of us would go home feeling like we’ve lost a piece of ourselves. Thank you for listening; it actually feels like the first time I’ve been able to say this out loud without being told it’s "just a phase."
